section.title: Idolatry in the Temple
section.sectionRationale: Carried by the Spirit in a vision to Jerusalem, Ezekiel is shown four escalating abominations, each one closer to the heart of the temple than the last, the idol of jealousy, secret animal worship by the elders, women weeping for Tammuz, and finally men bowing to the sun with their backs to the LORD's own sanctuary. God's glory is present to see it all.
section.thesis: God shows Ezekiel that Jerusalem's true crisis is not Babylon's army but corrupted worship at the very heart of the temple, idolatry growing bolder and more brazen the closer it draws to God's own house, provoking Him to withdraw pity from a people who have already driven Him out in their hearts.
section.frameNote: Four scenes of escalating abomination: the idol of jealousy at the north gate; the seventy elders offering incense to carved images in darkness; the women weeping for Tammuz at the temple's north gate; and the twenty five men bowing to the sun with their backs to the LORD, provoking His judgment.

units.label: Carried in vision to the idol of jealousy
units.note: the glory already seen, now confronted with rivals

verses.text: In the sixth year, on the fifth day of the sixth month, I was sitting in my house, and the elders of Judah were sitting before me; and there the hand of the Lord GOD fell upon me.
Then I looked and saw a figure like that of a man. From His waist down His appearance was like fire, and from His waist up He was as bright as the gleam of amber.

He stretched out what looked like a hand and took me by the hair of my head. Then the Spirit lifted me up between earth and heaven and carried me in visions of God to Jerusalem, to the entrance of the north gate of the inner court, where {{kanoh|the idol that provokes jealousy}} was seated.
And there I saw the glory of the God of Israel, like the vision I had seen in the plain.
“Son of man,” He said to me, “now lift up your eyes to the north.” So I lifted up my eyes to the north, and in the entrance north of the Altar Gate I saw this idol of jealousy.

“Son of man,” He said to me, “do you see what they are doing, the great {{toevah|abominations}} that the house of Israel is committing, to drive Me far from My sanctuary? Yet you will see even greater abominations.”

units.label: Seventy elders offering incense in the dark
units.note: the LORD does not see us, they say

verses.text: Then He brought me to the entrance to the court, and I looked and saw a hole in the wall.
“Son of man,” He told me, “dig through the wall.” So I dug through the wall and discovered a doorway.
Then He said to me, “Go in and see the wicked abominations they are committing here.”
So I went in and looked, and engraved all around the wall was every kind of crawling creature and detestable beast, along with all the idols of the house of Israel.
Before them stood seventy elders of the house of Israel, with Jaazaniah son of Shaphan standing among them. Each had a censer in his hand, and a fragrant cloud of incense was rising.

“Son of man,” He said to me, “do you see what the elders of the house of Israel are doing in the darkness, each at the shrine of his own idol? For they are saying, ‘The LORD does not see us; the LORD has forsaken the land.’”
Again, He told me, “You will see them committing even greater abominations.”

units.label: Women weeping for Tammuz
units.note: mourning a pagan god at the LORD's own gate

verses.text: Then He brought me to the entrance of the north gate of the house of the LORD, and I saw women sitting there, weeping for Tammuz.
“Son of man,” He said to me, “do you see this? Yet you will see even greater abominations than these.”

units.label: Backs to the temple, faces to the sun

verses.text: So He brought me to the inner court of the house of the LORD, and there at the entrance to the temple of the LORD, between the portico and the altar, were about twenty-five men with their backs to the temple of the LORD and their faces toward the east; and they were bowing to the east in worship of the sun.

“Son of man,” He said to me, “do you see this? Is it not enough for the house of Judah to commit the abominations they are practicing here, that they must also fill the land with violence and continually provoke Me to anger? Look, they are even putting the branch to their nose!
Therefore I will respond with wrath. I will not look on them with pity, nor will I spare them. Although they shout loudly in My ears, I will not listen to them.”

toevah.gloss: Abomination, something detestable that violates covenant order, most often used of idolatry and the practices that accompany it. The word recurs through this chapter as each successive vision is announced as a greater toevah than the last, building the case for judgment.

kanoh.gloss: The idol of jealousy, an image so named because it provokes God's covenant jealousy, His right to His people's exclusive worship, comparable to a husband's jealousy over an unfaithful spouse. Its placement near the temple entrance itself is a direct affront to the LORD enthroned within.

basic.context: Sitting in his own house with the elders of Judah before him, Ezekiel is seized by a vision and carried by the Spirit to Jerusalem, to the temple itself. There God shows him, in ascending order of horror, exactly why judgment is coming: an idol provoking jealousy at the temple's own gate, seventy elders secretly worshiping carved images in a hidden chamber, women publicly weeping for the pagan god Tammuz at the LORD's own gate, and finally twenty-five men standing with their backs to the temple, faces turned east, bowing to the rising sun. God's glory, the same glory Ezekiel saw by the Kebar, is present through it all, watching what His own people do in His own house.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a Spirit-given vision of real spiritual realities happening in Jerusalem's temple, using a guided tour structure, each abomination revealed as worse than the last, to make an escalating case for judgment. Readers should take the sequence and its emphasis on nearness to God's presence seriously as the theological point, rather than treating every visual detail as an isolated puzzle to be solved apart from that overall movement toward the holy of holies.
basic.originalLanguages: Each scene is called a toevah, an abomination, a word bound up with covenant violation and idolatry throughout the Old Testament, and the chapter piles up abomination upon abomination to justify what follows. The very first idol seen is remembered as provoking God's jealousy, His rightful claim to exclusive worship from a people bound to Him like a spouse.
basic.typology: The elders who say 'the LORD does not see us' anticipate every generation that presumes on God's apparent silence as though it were absence, while the vision's movement ever closer to the temple's inner sanctuary anticipates the New Testament's warning that true worship must be guarded at its very center, not merely at its edges.
basic.christ: Jesus, cleansing the temple and grieving that His Father's house of prayer had become a den of thieves, stands in the same tradition as this vision, confronting corrupted worship at its source, and His own body becomes the true temple, cleansed and pure, in contrast to the defiled sanctuary Ezekiel is shown here.
basic.god: God is present to see every hidden abomination, even incense burned in a secret chamber the elders assumed was dark enough to hide from Him, and He is provoked to righteous jealousy precisely because His people were made for exclusive, wholehearted worship of Him alone. His judgment, withholding pity, answers a people who first withdrew their own hearts from Him.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 20:5 grounds God's jealousy in the second commandment against idols. John 2:13-17 shows Jesus cleansing the temple of corrupted worship in righteous anger. Romans 1:25 describes the same pattern, worshiping the creature rather than the Creator, seen here in sun worship at the temple gate.
basic.oneStory: This is the anatomy of the corruption that finally justifies the temple's destruction and the exile, a people who filled God's own house with rival gods while assuming He could not see, foreshadowing every heart that quietly enthrones idols while still claiming God's name, and pointing to Christ, who alone cleanses and makes His people a true temple of worship.

context.literary: A carefully structured vision tour, four scenes each introduced by the phrase 'you will see even greater abominations,' building deliberately toward the climactic scene at the very entrance of the temple itself, directly setting up the departure of God's glory in the following chapters.
context.historical: Set in the sixth year of Ezekiel's exile, roughly two years before Jerusalem's fall, this vision exposes syncretistic worship practices, likely influenced by surrounding Canaanite, Mesopotamian, and Egyptian religion, that had crept into the very heart of Israelite worship despite earlier reforms under kings like Josiah.
context.canonical: This vision anticipates and explains the departure of God's glory from the temple in chapter 10, and it stands alongside other prophetic and later New Testament confrontations with corrupted worship, most notably Jesus' own cleansing of the temple.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the true depth and boldness of Jerusalem's idolatry, escalating from the temple's outer gate to its very inner court, so as to justify the coming judgment and the departure of God's glory that follows in the next chapters.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes specific historical idolatrous practices in Ezekiel's Jerusalem; it does not prescribe a checklist of particular ancient rituals to guard against today. Its abiding, prescriptive truth is that corrupted worship, however secret or seemingly small at first, tends to grow bolder and closer to the heart of true worship until it must be judged.

debates.question: Who or what was Tammuz, and what does the women's weeping signify?

views.label: A Mesopotamian fertility god mourned in an annual ritual
views.case: Tammuz was a Mesopotamian deity associated with vegetation and the seasonal cycle, mourned annually in a ritual lament for his mythological death, a practice that had apparently spread into Judah's own temple courts by Ezekiel's day.

views.label: A broader symbol of foreign religious syncretism
views.case: Whatever the precise mythological background, the scene's main force is that a pagan deity was being publicly mourned at the very gate of the LORD's house, showing how thoroughly foreign worship had been absorbed into supposedly Israelite religious life.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree the practice represents a serious breach of exclusive covenant worship, whatever the exact original mythology behind Tammuz, and both take the scene as evidence of deep-seated syncretism reaching into the temple itself.

study.originalLanguages: Every scene in this chapter is branded a toevah, an abomination, the strong covenant term for what utterly offends God's holiness, most often tied to idolatry. It begins with the semel ha-qinah, the idol of jealousy, a name that ties the whole vision back to the second commandment's warning that the LORD is a jealous God who will not share His people's worship with rivals.

typology.type: The elders who assume 'the LORD does not see us'
typology.fulfillment: Answered fully in the truth that nothing is hidden from God, Hebrews 4:13, and ultimately in Christ, who sees and cleanses hidden sin at its root, not merely its outward form.

typology.type: Idolatry advancing ever closer to the temple's inner sanctuary
typology.fulfillment: Anticipates Jesus' cleansing of the temple, John 2:13-17, and His teaching that true worship must be in spirit and truth, not merely in a sanctioned building, John 4:23-24.

study.christ: Centuries later, Jesus enters a temple again corrupted, not by carved idols in this instance but by commerce crowding out prayer, and He drives out what defiled His Father's house with the same righteous zeal this vision anticipates, John 2:13-17. Where the elders of Ezekiel 8 assumed the LORD could not see their secret idolatry, Jesus declares that nothing is hidden from the Father, and He alone can cleanse worship not merely at the temple's gate but at the human heart, making His people, by His Spirit, a true and living temple no longer capable of harboring hidden idols unchallenged.
study.god: God is present to see everything, the hidden incense chamber the elders thought was dark enough to hide their idolatry, the public weeping at His own gate, and the deliberate backs turned toward His temple. His jealousy is not petty possessiveness but the rightful claim of a covenant God whose people were made for undivided worship, and His refusal to show pity here answers a people who had already, in their hearts, driven Him far from His own sanctuary.

crossReferences.note: The second commandment grounds God's jealousy against idols, directly echoed by the idol of jealousy in this vision.
The direct sequel, where the glory of the LORD, provoked by this very idolatry, departs from the temple.
Jesus cleanses the temple of corrupted worship with the same righteous zeal this vision anticipates.
Describes exactly the pattern seen in the sun worship at the temple gate, worshiping the creature rather than the Creator.

study.oneStory: This is the moment the true depth of Jerusalem's corruption is exposed, idolatry advancing boldly from the temple gate to its very inner court while the people assume God cannot see. It explains why the glory must depart in the chapters that follow, and it points forward to Christ, who confronts corrupted worship at its root and makes His people, cleansed by His own blood, a temple where God's Spirit dwells without rival.

sermon.title: Corrupt Worship (Part 3)
sermon.why: Carson preaches through Ezekiel 8 and 9 together, tracing the escalating vision of idolatry in the temple that directly precedes and explains the judgment carried out by the six executioners and the departure of God's glory.

quiz.q: How was Ezekiel transported to see this vision of the temple?

options.text: He physically traveled by caravan from Babylon.
options.note: The transport is visionary, carried by the Spirit, not a literal journey.

options.text: A figure like a man seized him by the hair, and the Spirit carried him in visions of God to Jerusalem.
options.note: Ezekiel 8:2-3 describes this vivid means of transport in the vision.

options.text: He read about it in a letter from Jerusalem.
options.note: He is shown the scene directly in a vision, not through a letter.

options.text: The text does not describe how he arrived.
options.note: Verses 2-3 describe the means of transport explicitly.

quiz.q: What did the seventy elders say to justify their secret idolatry?

options.text: 'We are testing whether the idols have power.'
options.note: Their stated reasoning is about God's supposed absence, not an experiment.

options.text: They gave no explanation at all.
options.note: Their reasoning is quoted directly in verse 12.

options.text: 'The LORD does not see us; the LORD has forsaken the land.'
options.note: Ezekiel 8:12 records this exact justification.

options.text: 'The LORD commanded us to worship these images.'
options.note: They do not claim divine command; they assume God cannot see them.

quiz.q: What were the women doing at the north gate of the LORD's house?

options.text: Celebrating a harvest festival.
options.note: The scene is specifically weeping for a pagan deity, Tammuz.

options.text: Offering grain sacrifices to the LORD.
options.note: Their mourning was for a foreign god, not a sacrifice to the LORD.

options.text: Praying for the exiles' return.
options.note: The text specifies mourning for Tammuz, not prayer for exiles.

options.text: Weeping for Tammuz, a pagan deity.
options.note: Ezekiel 8:14 states this plainly.

quiz.q: What was the final and greatest abomination Ezekiel was shown?

options.text: About twenty-five men with their backs to the temple, bowing to the sun in the east.
options.note: Ezekiel 8:16 describes this climactic scene at the temple's inner court.

options.text: Children playing in the temple courts.
options.note: The final vision is specifically sun worship, not children playing.

options.text: Priests offering the correct sacrifices at the altar.
options.note: The scene depicts sun worship with backs turned to the LORD, not proper sacrifice.

options.text: Nothing further is shown after the weeping women.
options.note: A fourth, climactic scene follows in verses 16-18.

quiz.q: What does God say He will do in response to these abominations?

options.text: Overlook the idolatry because of Israel's past faithfulness.
options.note: The text says the opposite; God will not spare or show pity.

options.text: Immediately restore the temple to purity.
options.note: The chapter announces coming wrath, not immediate restoration.

options.text: Respond with wrath, showing no pity and refusing to listen even when they cry loudly to Him.
options.note: Ezekiel 8:18 states this response directly.

options.text: Give no response at all.
options.note: God's response of wrath is stated explicitly in verse 18.

questions: The elders assumed 'the LORD does not see us' while sinning in what they thought was darkness. Where might you be tempted to act as though God is not watching or does not care?

Each abomination in this vision draws closer to the temple's inner sanctuary than the last. How does sin in your own life tend to escalate gradually, moving closer to what matters most, if left unchecked?
The men in the final scene had their backs to the temple while facing the sun. What are you facing toward this week, God or some substitute, even while claiming to belong to Him?

God's glory was present through this entire vision, watching idolatry unfold in His own house. How does knowing God sees everything, even what feels hidden, change the way you think about private sin?
Jesus later cleanses a corrupted temple with righteous anger. How does knowing that Christ takes true worship this seriously shape the way you approach your own worship and devotion to Him?

translationNotes.note: The idol that provokes jealousy, sometimes rendered image of jealousy, is not named more specifically in the text; scholars have proposed various Canaanite deities, though its precise identity remains uncertain.

Jaazaniah son of Shaphan is named individually among the seventy elders, possibly significant since Shaphan's family elsewhere in Scripture is associated with support for reform under King Josiah, making this figure's presence among the idolaters especially striking.
Putting the branch to their nose describes an obscure ritual gesture whose exact significance is debated among scholars, though it clearly forms part of the condemned pagan worship practice.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session via the makor scripture toolkit for Ezekiel 8:1-18. No em or en dashes were present in the source text for this passage, and none have been introduced.
